summary: Gruber offers detailed observations from Apple's iPhone X event, covering Face ID, the notch, iPhone 8 improvements, the A11 chip, and AirPower charging. He defends Face ID against skeptics, noting Apple engineers using it daily say it 'just works,' while candidly criticizing the notch as ungainly, especially in landscape. He highlights the A11 Bionic chip as an unprecedented achievement that outperforms current MacBook Pros, and carefully explains the AirPower/Qi relationship as analogous to AirPods/Bluetooth. Despite reservations about the notch and naming, he's clearly enthusiastic about iPhone X as a major step forward.
key_points: Apple committed to Face ID over a year before launch, abandoning under-display Touch ID not because they couldn't do it but because they decided they didn't need it
The notch is a design compromise Gruber finds offensive—especially in landscape—but not a dealbreaker, comparing it to the camera bump
The A11 Bionic chip benchmarks faster than current MacBook Pros in both single and multi-core, an unprecedented situation where the best chips in computing are proprietary to one phone maker
AirPower is to Qi what AirPods are to Bluetooth: a superset with proprietary Apple enhancements, but unlike W1, Apple plans to offer improvements to the Qi consortium
iPhone 8 is a genuinely strong upgrade that Apple did not phone in, with True Tone displays, improved sensors, and much of the iPhone X's new technology
money_quotes: They stopped pursuing Touch ID under the display not because they couldn't do it, but because they decided they didn't need it.
When you go back to a Touch ID device, it feels broken that you have to touch the button to unlock the device.
I think Jony Ive either lost a bet or lost his mind.
Notch be damned, I know already that I would rather own an iPhone X than an iPhone 8 or 8 Plus.
Apple is pulling ahead of other chipmakers — both Intel and Qualcomm — like Secretariat pulling away from the pack in the Belmont Stakes.
Android users who want industry-leading performance are shit out of luck. This is unprecedented in computing history.
The specs aren't what matters — the effects are what matters. But the specs are what we can measure, and the faster the chips are, the better the effects are in the user experience.
The last perfect iPhone was the iPhone SE.
key_insight: The iPhone X represents Apple making bold, confident bets—Face ID over Touch ID, the notch as a necessary compromise—backed by unprecedented chip performance that gives them a proprietary advantage no competitor can match.
